s: Where the world's first million-pound cheque was signed, on a Cardiff trading floor
w: Built in 1888 on Mount Stuart Square, this Renaissance Revival hall was once the nerve centre of global coal. Up to 10,000 men streamed through daily at peak trading, shouting and gesticulating across the floor. In 1901 - some say 1904 - the world's first cheque for one million pounds was signed here. Cardiff's brief, dazzling zenith.
_facts: Built 1888 as Coal and Shipping Exchange for trading in South Wales coal
Renaissance Revival design by Edwin Seward, Butetown
Up to 10,000 people passed through daily at peak, 200 men on the floor at midday
First million-pound cheque signed here c.1901 (some sources say 1904)
Became music venue then closed for safety issues 2013; later redeveloped as hotel

s: Roald Dahl was baptised here; he later led the campaign to save it from ruin
w: Consecrated in 1868 for Scandinavian sailors working the Cardiff coal trade, this timber Lutheran chapel was dismantled in 1987, repaired in Norway, and reassembled on its current bay-front site in 1992. Roald Dahl - born nearby to Norwegian parents - was baptised here, and championed its rescue when it fell derelict in the 1970s. The Dahl Gallery inside is named in his memory.
_facts: Consecrated 1868 by the Norwegian Seamen's Mission for Scandinavian sailors in Cardiff
Roald Dahl (born Cardiff 1916 to Norwegian parents) was baptised here along with his sisters
Dahl led fundraising campaign in the 1970s to save the church from disrepair
Building dismantled, repaired, and re-erected on current Cardiff Bay site in 1992
Now operates as Norwegian Church Arts Centre with a Dahl Gallery

s: The world's oldest record shop, trading since 1894 - started with wax cylinders
w: Henry Spiller opened this shop in 1894, originally selling gramaphone novelties and wax cylinders. It migrated across Cardiff's arcades over the decades - wax to shellac to vinyl - before settling in the ornate Morgan Arcade in 2010. Still independent, still playing loud. No streaming service can claim 130-odd years.
_facts: Established 1894 by Henry Spiller, recognised as the world's oldest record shop
Originally sold wax cylinders and early gramaphone products
Location history: Queens Arcade, then The Hayes, now Morgan Arcade since 2010
Currently at 27 Morgan Arcade, operating on two floors
Morgan Arcade itself dates to the late 1890s

s: Cardiff's Baby Big Ben: the dock company's HQ whose original clockwork was sold to an American
w: Raised in 1897 for the Bute Dock Company on a site where its predecessor burned down in 1892, this terracotta French-Gothic confection bristles with gargoyles and hexagonal chimneys. Its clock earned the nickname 'Baby Big Ben of Wales.' The original 1897 mechanism was auctioned by British Rail in 1973 and bought by an American collector; returned to Cardiff in 2005, it now lives in the city centre as a public sculpture.
_facts: Built 1897 as headquarters for the Bute Dock Company, replacing a building destroyed by fire in 1892
Designed by William Frame in French-Gothic Renaissance style with glazed terracotta from J.C. Edwards of Ruabon
Clock tower nicknamed 'Baby Big Ben' or 'Big Ben of Wales'
Original 1897 clock mechanism (by William Potts & Sons, Leeds) sold to an American in 1973 by British Rail
Clock returned to Cardiff 2005 and reinstalled as public art by Marianne Forrest in 2011
Grade I listed building

s: Fourteen stone beasts with glass eyes line a city-centre wall, designed by Burges who died before seeing them built
w: William Burges dreamed up these fourteen creatures in the 1860s for the third Marquess of Bute, but died in 1881 before a single stone was cut. His sculptor Thomas Nicholls carved the first nine - bears, hyenas, a seal, apes, lions - from 1887 to 1889, each given individually coloured glass eyes. Six more, slightly chunkier, were added in the 1930s. They were originally painted. Today they line Castle Street in weathered grey, watching traffic.
_facts: Designed by William Burges in 1860s for third Marquess of Bute; Burges died 1881 before execution
Original nine animals carved by Thomas Nicholls 1887-1889: apes, bear, hyena, lions, lioness, lynx, seal, wolf
Animals originally painted in naturalistic colours (paintwork later removed)
Six additional animals added by Alexander Carrick for the fourth Marquess when wall was relocated 1930s
Grade I listed structure fronting Bute Park on Castle Street

s: 13th-century Dominican friary sacked by Owain Glyndwr, dissolved by Henry VIII, rediscovered under a park
w: Founded in 1256, one of only two Dominican friaries in Wales, this complex was torched by Owain Glyndwr's forces in 1404, rebuilt, then dissolved by Henry VIII in 1538. The stones were carted off and the footprint disappeared under soil for three centuries. The third Marquess had it excavated in 1887 and 1897; today the full medieval plan is traced in grass in Bute Park, one of the few friary sites where the complete layout is known.
_facts: Dominican friary founded 1256 in Cardiff; one of only two Dominican houses in Wales
Destroyed in 1404 during Owain Glyndwr's attack on Cardiff, later rebuilt
Dissolved by Henry VIII 1538 and demolished; stones removed for use elsewhere
Third Marquess of Bute had foundations excavated in 1887 and 1897
One of few friary sites where the complete building plan is known; scheduled monument

s: A miniature lighthouse on a lake, built to remember the Antarctic voyage that departed Cardiff in 1910
w: Captain Scott's ship Terra Nova sailed from Cardiff Docks on 15 June 1910, never to return. The city commemorated the lost expedition with this small clock tower-lighthouse on the lake in Roath Park, completed in 1915 at a cost of under 160 pounds. The wooden figurehead from Terra Nova stands on the southern bank. On a grey Welsh day, the whole scene - toy lighthouse, grey water, vanished voyage - is quietly devastating.
_facts: Terra Nova departed Cardiff Docks 15 June 1910; Scott and four companions died on the return from the South Pole in 1912
The wooden figurehead from SS Terra Nova was presented to Cardiff by ship owner Frederick Charles Bowring in December 1913
Lighthouse clock tower built 1914-1915 at cost of 159 pounds 6 shillings 8 pence
Officially presented to the City on 14 October 1918
Located on the north end of Roath Park Lake in Cardiff

s: Cardiff's oldest shopping arcade - and its first lending library was in a room above the entrance
w: Opened in 1858, Cardiff's first indoor arcade displaced slum housing on medieval burgage plots to link St Mary Street to The Hayes. Its glass roof on fretted iron ribs became a template for the city's later covered passages. Three years after it opened, a voluntary lending library was established above the entrance - Cardiff's first public reading room. The School of Art followed in rooms above the shops; students included sculptor Goscombe John.
_facts: Opened 1858, oldest surviving shopping arcade in Cardiff; Grade II listed building
Designed by architects James and Price; replaced overcrowded slum housing on medieval burgage plots
In 1861 a free lending library was established above the St Mary Street entrance
Cardiff School of Art later used rooms above the arcade; students included sculptor Goscombe John
Single glass roof on fretted iron ribs; early example of covered retail design in Wales

s: A coal-merchant's neo-Gothic mansion whose tower was built to outshine Cardiff Castle
w: James Harvey Insole made his fortune shipping South Wales coal and in the 1870s hired Edwin Seward - the architect who also built the Coal Exchange - to dress his modest villa in Gothic Revival stone. The clock tower added in 1874 deliberately echoed William Burges's work on Cardiff Castle next door to the same patron's estate. The Insole family lived here until 1938; Cardiff Council bought the 57 acres for 26,250 pounds under compulsory purchase in 1932.
_facts: Original building 1855 designed by W.G. & E. Habershon for James Harvey Insole, coal-shipper and mine-owner
Remodelled in Gothic Revival style 1870s by George Robinson and Edwin Seward
Neo-Gothic tower added 1874, echoing Burges's clock tower at Cardiff Castle
Insole family occupied the estate from 1856 to 1938
Cardiff Council bought the 57-acre estate for 26,250 pounds in 1932; Grade II* listed

s: A Nazi parachute mine destroyed the nave in 1941; Jacob Epstein's aluminium Christ rose above it in 1957
w: On 2 January 1941 a landmine on a parachute snagged the cathedral spire and fell into the graveyard, tearing off the nave roof and shattering the windows. Architect George Pace rebuilt the nave around a double-wishbone concrete arch, and in 1957 placed on it Jacob Epstein's sixteen-foot aluminium Christ in Majesty - Epstein had waived part of his fee to cast it in aluminium rather than gilded plaster. Norman origins, Blitz ruin, mid-century audacity.
_facts: Cathedral's Norman origins date to Bishop Urban c.1120
On 2 January 1941 a parachute landmine snagged the spire, fell into the graveyard and destroyed much of the nave roof and furnishings
Rebuilt by architect George Pace with a concrete double-wishbone arch
Jacob Epstein's 16-foot Christ in Majesty (aluminium) installed 1957; Epstein waived part of his fee to cast in aluminium not plaster
Nave rehallowed 10 April 1957; cathedral is Grade I listed
Roald Dahl attended the Cathedral School here as a child

s: Wales's national concert hall was shoehorned on top of a half-built shopping centre
w: When Cardiff finally resolved to build a proper concert hall in the late 1970s, the only available site was already half-occupied by the foundations of a new St David's Shopping Centre. Architects solved the problem by building the hall on top of - and wrapped around - the existing structure. The result opened in 1982 with a famously dry acoustic, described as 'complex late brutalism.' Home of the BBC Cardiff Singer of the World competition.
_facts: Public desire for a concert hall dated to the 1950s; built in only five years
Architects had to fit the hall into and on top of a partly built St David's Shopping Centre due to cramped site
First concert held 11 September 1982; officially opened 15 February 1983 by the Queen Mother
Building Design magazine described it as 'complex late brutalism'
National Concert Hall of Wales; home of annual Welsh Proms and biennial BBC Cardiff Singer of the World

s: Born at Llwyn-yr-eos in 1893, the boy from Canton wrote the song that sustained the Western Front
w: David Ivor Davies was born here on 15 January 1893, son of a Welsh singing teacher who named the house Llwyn-yr-eos - Grove of the Nightingales. He reinvented himself as Ivor Novello, and in 1914 wrote 'Keep the Home Fires Burning,' the anthem that became second only to 'Tipperary' for Allied troops. A blue plaque marks the arched doorway; the house is otherwise unremarkably residential.
_facts: Ivor Novello (David Ivor Davies) born 15 January 1893 at Llwyn-yr-eos (Grove of the Nightingales), Cowbridge Road East, Canton
His mother Clara Novello Davies was a renowned singer and founder of the Welsh Ladies Choir
Wrote 'Keep the Home Fires Burning' in 1914, one of the most popular songs of World War I
Blue plaque on the garden wall marks the birthplace
Family later moved to 11 Cathedral Road, Cardiff

s: Built on the site of Cardiff Gaol, where Wales's most controversial martyr was hanged in 1831
w: The market entrance on St Mary Street stands directly over the spot where the gallows of Cardiff Gaol once stood. On 13 August 1831, Richard Lewis - Dic Penderyn - was hanged here for allegedly stabbing a soldier during the Merthyr Rising, despite over 11,000 signatures demanding his reprieve. In 1874 a man named Ianto Parker confessed on his deathbed in America to committing the stabbing. A plaque inside marks the site.
_facts: Cardiff Market built 1891 on former site of Cardiff Gaol designed by Borough Surveyor William Harpur
Gallows on St Mary Street entrance were where Dic Penderyn (Richard Lewis, aged 23) was hanged 13 August 1831
Dic Penderyn convicted of stabbing a soldier during Merthyr Rising of June 1831; over 11,000 petitioned for reprieve
In 1874 Ianto Parker confessed on his deathbed in the United States to being the real stabber
Plaque to Dic Penderyn located inside the market near the gallows site

s: The only medieval building in Cardiff city centre apart from the castle, its tower rising 130 feet above St Mary Street
w: Founded around 1180 as a chapel of ease, this church was wrecked by Owain Glyndwr in 1404 and largely rebuilt in the second half of the 15th century - its 130-foot Perpendicular Gothic tower is the one surviving medieval landmark in Cardiff's retail core. Fittings include the Bute family's marble sarcophagi and an ornate Victorian north aisle added as their mausoleum in the 1880s. Grade I listed and easy to miss entirely.
_facts: Founded c.1180 as a chapel of ease; present nave and tower rebuilt mid-15th century
Badly damaged during Owain Glyndwr's 1404 rebellion
130-foot Perpendicular Gothic west tower is the only medieval building in Cardiff city centre other than Cardiff Castle
Bute family mausoleum added 1881-1886 with seven red granite sarcophagi
Grade I listed building

s: A druid stone circle built for an 1899 Eisteddfod, relocated to make way for City Hall, now surrounded by civic buildings
w: For the 1899 National Eisteddfod held in Cathays Park, a ring of upright stones with white alabaster recumbent markers was erected as a permanent Gorsedd circle - the ritual space of Welsh bardic ceremony. When the City Hall went up in 1905 the whole circle was dismantled and re-erected in what became Gorsedd Gardens. Originally labelled the 'Druidical Gardens.' Still used for Eisteddfod ceremony when the festival returns to Cardiff.
_facts: Stone circle erected 1899 for National Eisteddfod of Wales held in Cathays Park, Cardiff
Circle originally placed in field next to Eisteddfod pavilion; moved to Gorsedd Gardens in 1905 to make way for City Hall
Garden originally known as 'Druidical Gardens'; renamed Gorsedd Gardens
Circle features upright stones and white alabaster recumbent stones
Officially opened to public July 1910; still used for Gorsedd of Welsh Bards ceremonies

s: Cardiff's Victorian jubilee park was home to a seal named Billy for 27 years
w: Created on Ely Common in 1897-98 to mark Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee, this Canton park acquired an unlikely resident in 1912: a rescued common seal, named Billy, who lived in the ornamental lake until his death in 1939. His 27-year tenancy made him famous enough to merit a bronze memorial installed in 1997, and he's still the park's defining mascot. Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show also stopped here in 1891.
_facts: Victoria Park created 1897-98 on Ely Common for Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee
In 1912 a rescued seal named Billy was given a home in the ornamental lake
Billy the seal lived in the park for 27 years until his death in 1939
A sculpture commemorating Billy was installed in the park in 1997
Original cast iron bandstand installed February 1897; replica installed 1996
Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show visited in 1891

s: A working Victorian fairy-tale castle built on a real medieval ruin - and never actually lived in
w: Gilbert de Clare built a stone castle here in the 1260s to hold his freshly annexed Welsh lands. It crumbled to rubble over centuries. In 1875 the third Marquess of Bute - the same patron behind Cardiff Castle's interiors - commissioned William Burges to rebuild it entirely as a medieval fantasy. Burges died in 1881; the work was completed posthumously. The Marquess almost never stayed. Today it rises above the M4 like an apparition.
_facts: Original medieval castle built by Gilbert de Clare c.1267-1277 to control Welsh lands
Reduced to rubble over centuries; third Marquess commissioned William Burges to rebuild 1875
Burges died 1881 before completion; work continued under his former assistants
High Victorian Gothic Revival design; considered 'the crowning glory of the Gothic Revival' in Britain by historian Charles Kightly
Third Marquess rarely visited despite the cost; now managed by Cadw

s: Richard Rogers won the competition to house Wales's new parliament; the funnel in the roof is also a chimney
w: Opened by the Queen on St David's Day 2006 at a cost of 69.6 million pounds, the Welsh Parliament building resulted from an international design competition won by Richard Rogers Partnership. The sweeping slate-and-glass structure has a distinctive funnel rising from the debating chamber - part skylight, part natural ventilation stack. Freely open to the public; entry to the Senedd chamber is without charge or appointment.
_facts: Opened 1 March 2006 (St David's Day) by Queen Elizabeth II at a cost of 69.6 million pounds
Designed by Richard Rogers Partnership (now Rogers Stirk Harbour + Partners) through international competition
Funnel/chimney structure allows natural light into chamber and acts as ventilation
Freely accessible to the public without charge; houses the Welsh Parliament (Senedd Cymru)
Located on the former coal dock estate at Cardiff Bay

s: Free to enter and holding the Morse code slip from the world's first over-sea wireless message
w: Chartered in 1907 and finally opened in 1922 after delays caused by the First World War, this neoclassical Cathays Park institution holds Wales's natural history, art and archaeology under one roof for free. Among its stranger holdings: the Morse recording slip from Marconi's 1897 transmission from Flat Holm to Lavernock Point - the first wireless message sent over open sea - and the world's largest leatherback turtle, found beached at Harlech in 1988 at 916 kg.
_facts: Royal charter granted 1907; building opened 1922 after WWI delayed construction begun 1912
Admission free; houses art, natural history and archaeology for Wales
Holds the Morse code recording slip from Marconi's first over-sea wireless transmission, 13 May 1897 (Flat Holm to Lavernock Point)
Displays the world's largest leatherback turtle (916 kg, nearly 3m long) found at Harlech in 1988
The Clore Discovery Centre gives access to 7.5 million items usually in storage

s: The UK's first retractable-roof stadium, built for 1999 Rugby World Cup on the site of the Wales Empire Pool
w: The Wales Empire Pool - Cardiff's first modernist building, purpose-built for the 1958 British Empire Games - was demolished in 1998 to make way for this 73,931-seat colossus. It became the UK's first retractable-roof stadium, the roof taking 20 minutes to travel its full span. The acoustic insulation is deliberate: closed, the bowl traps crowd noise until the roar is almost physical. The Wembley scenes in '28 Weeks Later' were filmed here.
_facts: Site previously held the Wales Empire Pool (1958-1998), designed for the 1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games and Cardiff's first modernist building
Empire Pool demolished 1998 to make way for the Millennium Stadium (now Principality Stadium)
Stadium capacity 73,931; opened for 1999 Rugby World Cup
First retractable roof in the UK; roof takes 20 minutes to open or close
Interior scenes of Wembley in the film '28 Weeks Later' (2007) were shot here
Renamed Principality Stadium in 2016 under WRU sponsorship deal
